intro: No European capital does ancient trees quite like London. Its oldest oaks survive largely thanks to royal appetite: kings walled off huge hunting parks for their deer, and the trees inside were left to grow old while the countryside around them was cleared. The city even gave its name to the London plane, the soot-proof hybrid that lines its streets. These ten range from a churchyard yew older than Londinium itself to a plane tree once valued at three quarters of a million pounds, and all but one are free to visit.
meta_description: London's ancient trees hide in royal parks and churchyards. Start with the Totteridge Yew, alive since before the Romans built Londinium.
question_answer: The oldest tree in London is the Totteridge Yew, a European yew in St Andrew's churchyard in Totteridge, North London, estimated at between 1,000 and 2,000 years old. It is the oldest living thing in the city, and it stands a 15 minute walk from Totteridge and Whetstone station on the Northern Line.
question_meta: The Totteridge Yew, up to 2,000 years old, is London's oldest tree. Where it stands, how to reach it, and the full ten of the city's ancient trees.
question_context: Ring counting does not work on ancient yews because their heartwood rots away, so estimates for the Totteridge Yew run from 1,000 to 2,000 years. Even the cautious low end makes it older than any building in London, and the high end means it was already a substantial tree when the Romans founded Londinium. It stands in the churchyard of St Andrew's in Totteridge, a village long since swallowed by North London, and the 13th century church beside it is the newcomer on the plot: the tree predates it by centuries at minimum. The district's Hundred Court once met in its shade, which tells you how long it has been the local landmark. London's runners-up are oaks rather than yews: Richmond Park and Greenwich Park hold hollow pollards in the 700 to 900 year range, survivors of the royal hunting grounds. But for sheer age, nothing in the capital touches the yew. Getting there is simple: Totteridge and Whetstone station on the Northern Line, then a 15 minute walk through the old village.

faq.q: What is the oldest tree in London?
faq.a: The Totteridge Yew in St Andrew's churchyard, Totteridge, estimated at between 1,000 and 2,000 years old. Even the cautious low estimate makes it older than any building in the city.

faq.q: Are London's ancient trees free to visit?
faq.a: Nine of the ten trees on this list are free: they stand in public parks, churchyards and squares. The exception is the Old Lions of Kew, which need a Kew Gardens ticket.

faq.q: Why does London have so many ancient trees?
faq.a: Royal hunting parks. Kings walled off Richmond and Greenwich centuries ago, and the oaks inside were spared the clearing that transformed the surrounding countryside.
A hybrid plane tree that thrives on soot and hard pruning. London planted it everywhere from the 1700s on, and the tree took the city's name with it.

trees.name: The Totteridge Yew

location.address: St Andrew's Churchyard, Totteridge Village, London N20 8PR

trees.story: The oldest living thing in London. This yew was already ancient when the Romans were building Londinium two thousand years ago. The church beside it was not built until the 13th century, meaning the tree is over a millennium older than the building that now stands next to it. Its trunk is hollow and propped, its girth nearly eight metres. The Hundred Court of the district once met in its shadow. It has been pollarded many times over the centuries, which is partly why it is still alive.
trees.transport: 15 min walk from Totteridge & Whetstone station (Northern Line)

trees.name: The Royal Oak

location.address: Near Pen Ponds, Richmond Park, Richmond, London

trees.story: The wall came second: this oak was already centuries old when Charles I enclosed Richmond Park around it in 1637. It is a massive hollow pollard, its trunk split and gnarled, wide enough to shelter a small football team, and it has lived behind royal fences ever since the King claimed the land for his deer. Richmond Park holds one of the largest collections of ancient oaks in Europe, and the Royal Oak is its figurehead. Sir David Attenborough made it the symbol of a conservation campaign. The deer still graze around it as they have for centuries.
trees.transport: Bus or car to Richmond Park, then on foot near Pen Ponds

trees.name: The Cheapside Plane

location.address: Corner of Wood Street and Cheapside, City of London, EC2V 8AA

trees.story: The only Great Tree of London in the Square Mile. It grows in what was the churchyard of St Peter Cheap, a church destroyed in the Great Fire of 1666 and never rebuilt. No one knows exactly how old it is. By 1853, a cab company was advertising its location as 'Under the tree, Cheapside.' In 1901 it was called the most valuable tree in the world. It survived the Blitz. Buildings beside it are prohibited from rising above two storeys by the terms of their leases, preserving its canopy. Wordsworth likely had it in mind in his 1800 poem Poor Susan.
trees.access: Free, public square, City of London
trees.transport: 2 min walk from St Paul's tube station
trees.notes: Source URLs need backfilling (original research cited Atlas Obscura and Wikimedia Commons without links).

trees.name: The Berkeley Square Plane
trees.story: In 2008 one of the plane trees in Berkeley Square was valued at 750,000 pounds, briefly making it the most valuable tree in Britain. The square's planes were planted in 1789 by MP Edward Bouverie, who lived at number 13, and are among the oldest in London. The nightingale that sang in Berkeley Square was almost certainly singing from one of these branches. They were among the original 41 Great Trees of London, and the Grade II listed garden they shade has kept its shape while everything around it changed.
trees.transport: 2 min walk from Green Park or Bond Street tube

trees.name: Queen Elizabeth's Oak
trees.label: Fallen monument

location.address: Near Maze Hill Gate, Lover's Walk, Greenwich Park, London SE10

trees.story: This tree fell during a storm in 1991 after standing since the 12th century. Its hollowed carcass still lies where it fell. Henry VIII was born in Greenwich Palace and is said to have danced around this oak with Anne Boleyn while courting. Their daughter Elizabeth I, also born here, reportedly picnicked under its canopy, giving the tree her name. The hollow trunk was allegedly used as a lock-up for troublemakers. It is no longer alive, but its remains are worth visiting.
trees.transport: Cutty Sark DLR station, 15 min walk
trees.notes: Tree is dead and recumbent since 1991. Ruins only. Labelled 'Fallen monument' on the city page so visitors know what to expect. Source URLs need backfilling.

trees.name: The Mulberry of Charlton House

location.address: Charlton House, Charlton Road, London SE7 8RE

trees.story: Planted around 1608 as part of King James I's ill-fated plan to establish a British silk industry. He ordered mulberry trees planted across England to feed silkworms. The plan failed because he accidentally ordered black mulberries, whose leaves silkworms refuse to eat. This tree, one of the survivors of that royal blunder, is now over 400 years old. It leans and sprawls dramatically, held up by wooden supports, and still produces fruit every summer.
trees.access: Free, open grounds of Charlton House community centre
trees.notes: Source URLs need backfilling (original research cited Morus Londinium).

trees.name: The Fulham Palace Oak

location.address: Fulham Palace Gardens, Bishop's Avenue, London SW6 6EA

trees.story: This oak is five centuries old partly because people kept cutting it back. It was coppiced long ago, harvested down to its base, a practice that paradoxically resets a tree's clock and can extend its life by centuries. It grows in the grounds of Fulham Palace, the former home of the Bishop of London. Bishop Grindal, who lived here in the 16th century, is credited with introducing the tamarisk to England and growing grapes sent to Queen Elizabeth I. The oak was among the original 41 trees awarded Great Tree status in 1988.
trees.access: Free, Fulham Palace Gardens open daily
trees.notes: Original research marked this tree as needing photo verification. Source URLs need backfilling.

trees.name: The Old Lions of Kew
trees.species: Multiple species including Japanese Pagoda Tree (Styphnolobium japonicum)

location.address: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew, Richmond, London TW9 3AE

trees.story: Five of the oldest trees in Kew Gardens, nicknamed the Old Lions by the garden's first curator John Smith. Among them is a Japanese pagoda tree planted in 1762, making it one of the oldest in Europe outside its native range. Kew's collection includes trees that have been growing here since the garden opened under Princess Augusta in the 1750s. Entry to Kew required but worth it.
trees.transport: Kew Gardens tube station (District Line), 5 min walk

trees.name: The Evelyn Mulberry

location.address: Sayes Court Park, Sayes Court Street, Deptford, London SE8 5AB

trees.story: Sayes Court Garden was the celebrated 17th century garden of writer and diarist John Evelyn, one of the most famous gardens in England. The garden was destroyed when Tsar Peter the Great of Russia rented the house in 1698 and reportedly used a wheelbarrow to race through the hedges for amusement. This mulberry may have survived even that. A stone at its base commemorates the story. Most of the garden is gone, but this tree, fenced and propped, still stands in what is now an unremarkable public park.
trees.transport: Deptford or Deptford Bridge station, 5 min walk
trees.notes: Source URLs need backfilling (original research cited Atlas Obscura).

trees.name: Barney the Plane

location.address: Barn Elms, Rocks Lane, Barnes, London SW13

trees.story: The biggest London plane in London is a tree most Londoners have never heard of. Nicknamed Barney, it is over 300 years old, potentially the oldest of its kind in the city, and massive in every dimension. The London plane is the capital's signature tree, chosen for its resilience to soot and its tolerance of brutal pruning, and Barney is the patriarch of them all. It grows at Barn Elms in the riverside suburb of Barnes. One honest caveat: the exact spot is still being verified, which is why this tree sits at the bottom of the list until its pin can be trusted.
trees.notes: Moved to position 10 pending verification (2026-07-19 quality round). Next nightly run: confirm exact coordinates within Barn Elms via two sources and research a tree-specific story to replace the generic London plane material.
